attack_surface.exposure: Walks Path directory for *.test.goal, reads each file's .pr and .goal text, recurses static goal.call chains.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: User's own project tree during --test. No network/untrusted data ingress.
gaps: ValidatePath is string-prefix based; symlink-escape is an existing FS-wrapper concern, not new here

attack_surface.exposure: Spawns fresh App per test with own MemoryStack/Goals/FileSystem rooted at TestFile.Directory; timeout via linked CancellationTokenSource; parallel semaphore.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Each child App has its own root; SystemDirectory inherited from parent.
mitigations: Coverage uses ConcurrentDictionary and Merge is idempotent
gaps: AfterAction coverage binding captures childApp by closure — disposed-timing is fine because binding only lives on childApp.User.Context.Events

attack_surface.exposure: Writes console summary, JSON (results.json) or JUnit XML (junit.xml) under .test/; surfaces file content in Data.Properties.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Output consumed by CI and dev terminals. Content sourced from test errors + captured stdout.
mitigations: SecurityElement.Escape for XML attributes and bodies
Regex strip of ANSI CSI sequences in captured output
gaps: ANSI strip regex only covers CSI (\x1B[); misses OSC (\x1B]), DCS (\x1BP), charset designators (\x1B(B) — OSC-8 hyperlink forging or terminal-title manipulation still possible via a failing test's stdout

SecurityElement.Escape does not strip invalid-for-XML C0 controls (\x00-\x08, \x0B-\x0C, \x0E-\x1F) — a failing test whose error message contains raw control bytes produces XML that strict JUnit parsers reject (DoS-of-reporting, not security)

attack_surface.exposure: On assert failure, Variables.Snapshot() captures all user variables (excluding !-infra, DynamicData, SettingsVariable) into the error; flows into test.report JSON output and into the console failure block.
mitigations: Excludes SettingsVariable (backed by settings store, not printed)
gaps: Ordinary user variables may hold secrets (HTTP bearer tokens, DB connection strings, response bodies with PII) — these WILL appear verbatim in a failing test's artefact. By design for diagnostics, but downstream publishing of results.json/junit.xml should be treated as sensitive.

attack_surface.exposure: Reads untyped IDictionary<string,object?> from CLI parsing into Testing.Apply.
mitigations: Apply enforces bounds (timeout>0, parallel>0, format∈{json,junit}) and returns typed ConfigError

attack_surface.exposure: Multi-action step with condition.* actions is orchestrated by the head if; branch metadata is attached to Data.Properties and consumed by coverage.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Internal Context flow, no external data.
mitigations: Guard key stored on Context (not Variables) so PLang user can't overwrite
gaps: Step.GetHashCode is identity-based in .NET (System.Runtime.CompilerServices.RuntimeHelpers) — collisions are theoretically possible but benign: worst case is a sibling step sharing a hash would see alreadyOrchestrating=true and skip orchestration, degrading to simple path. Not a security issue.

findings.vector: Terminal escape injection via captured test stdout rendered in failure blocks.
findings.preconditions: A test runs under --test and produces captured stdout containing OSC/DCS escape sequences (e.g. OSC 8 hyperlink, OSC 0 window title). Test fails → test.report renders the stdout inline via StripAnsi which only matches CSI. Attacker-controlled content reaching a test's stdout (e.g. an echo of an HTTP response body) could forge hyperlinks or hijack the terminal title when the CI log viewer renders the console output.
findings.impact: Spoofed clickable URLs in terminal output, manipulated window title. Confused-deputy for a developer reading test logs.
findings.exploit_sketch: Fixture that writes "\x1B]8;;https://evil.example/\x07click me\x1B]8;;\x07" to stdout, then fails an assert. Render pipeline preserves the OSC because StripAnsi regex `\x1B\[[0-?]*[ -/]*[@-~]` only matches CSI (`\x1B[...`).
findings.proposed_fix: Broaden the regex to strip OSC (`\x1B\][^\x07\x1B]*(?:\x07|\x1B\\)`), DCS/APC/PM/SOS, and charset designators, OR blanket-strip 0x1B entirely from captured output before rendering. Same treatment should apply to console rendering of AssertionError.Actual/Expected strings.

findings.vector: JUnit/JSON report may contain invalid XML 1.0 control characters, breaking strict parsers.
findings.preconditions: Test error message or captured output contains C0 control bytes (e.g. \x00 from a binary comparison failure, \x01–\x1F from raw-bytes assertions).
findings.impact: CI JUnit ingester (Jenkins, GitHub Actions junit-reporter) rejects the whole file → test results invisible. Denial-of-reporting, not a security boundary break.
findings.exploit_sketch: Assert on `new byte[] {0x00}` → AssertionError.Actual stringified contains \x00 → <failure>…\x00…</failure> written verbatim (SecurityElement.Escape only covers <>&"').
findings.proposed_fix: Pre-filter error message and captured output through a C0-stripper (allow only \t, \n, \r; drop 0x00-0x08, 0x0B-0x0C, 0x0E-0x1F) before XML escape.

findings.vector: Variables snapshot on assertion failure may serialize secrets into results.json.
findings.preconditions: A failing test's MemoryStack holds user variables with sensitive content (API token loaded via http.request, row fetched from a users table, etc.). Testing.Apply does not scrub the snapshot. Report artefact uploaded to shared storage.
findings.impact: Secret exfiltration into CI artefacts. Responsibility-relative severity: user owns their vars; PLang's job is to be conservative about what ends up in failure diagnostics.
findings.exploit_sketch: Existing [Sensitive] attribute system marks specific Data values. Snapshot() does not check it — all non-!, non-dynamic, non-SettingsVariable vars are included raw.
findings.proposed_fix: Honor [Sensitive] in Variables.Snapshot(): replace values of Sensitive-flagged Data with a placeholder token (e.g. "(sensitive)"). Also consider redacting values whose type hint is `password` / `token` / `secret`. Document the leak surface in test.report header.

findings.vector: test.discover recursion depth cap of 50 protects goal.call chains but ExtractAutoTags and SeedBranchChains each have their own visited set — a pathological cyclic graph with 50+ distinct nodes forcing 50-deep recursion twice costs O(N×steps×actions).
findings.preconditions: User's own .pr tree. Since this runs only under --test on the user's machine, not a real attack.
findings.impact: CPU burn at discovery time. Bounded, terminates.
findings.exploit_sketch: N/A — within user's own trust domain.
findings.proposed_fix: No fix required. Noting for the record.

summary: Test module is opt-in local/CI tooling with a narrow attack surface. Isolation (child App per test, linked CTS, ConcurrentQueue Results, ConcurrentDictionary Coverage) is correct. Main concerns are defense-in-depth on output rendering (incomplete ANSI strip, no C0 filter) and an info-disclosure caveat on Variables snapshots in failure artefacts — all low severity given the threat model (user-owned test runs, user-owned vars). No critical/high findings. Verdict: pass. Suggest auditor next.
